beginner.question: What is web accessibility?
beginner.answer: Web accessibility means designing and developing websites that can be used by people with disabilities, including visual, auditory, physical, speech, cognitive, and neurological disabilities. It ensures equal access to information and functionality.

beginner.question: What are the four principles of WCAG?
beginner.answer: POUR: Perceivable (users can perceive content through multiple senses), Operable (users can operate the interface), Understandable (content and interface are understandable), Robust (content works with current and future assistive technologies).

beginner.question: What is the difference between WCAG Level A, AA, and AAA?
beginner.answer: Level A is the minimum accessibility requirements. Level AA is the standard legal requirement, addressing the most common barriers. Level AAA is the highest level but may not be achievable for all content.

beginner.question: Why is alt text important for images?
beginner.answer: Alt text provides a text alternative for images that screen readers can announce. It describes the image content or function, allowing blind users to understand what the image conveys.

beginner.question: What are semantic HTML elements and why are they important?
beginner.answer: Semantic elements (nav, main, article, section, header, footer) describe their purpose. They provide built-in accessibility by defining landmarks that screen readers use for navigation.

beginner.question: What is keyboard accessibility?
beginner.answer: Keyboard accessibility means all functionality can be operated using only a keyboard. Users should be able to Tab through interactive elements, activate them with Enter/Space, and navigate with arrow keys.

beginner.question: How do you make a form accessible?
beginner.answer: Use label elements associated with inputs via for/id. Provide clear instructions, error messages, and proper fieldset/legend grouping. Use aria-required for required fields and aria-describedby for help text.

beginner.question: What is a skip link?
beginner.answer: A skip link is a keyboard-accessible link at the top of the page that allows users to jump directly to the main content, bypassing repetitive navigation links.

beginner.question: What is the minimum color contrast ratio for normal text?
beginner.answer: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18px bold or 24px regular). This ensures readability for users with low vision.

beginner.question: What is a focus indicator?
beginner.answer: A focus indicator is a visible outline or highlight that shows which element currently has keyboard focus. It's essential for keyboard navigation.

intermediate.question: How do you test a website for accessibility?
intermediate.answer: Use a combination of automated tools (WAVE, axe, Lighthouse) and manual testing: navigate with keyboard only, test with screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver), zoom to 200%, disable CSS, check color contrast with tools, and review against WCAG criteria.

intermediate.question: What is ARIA and when should you use it?
intermediate.answer: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) provides attributes to enhance accessibility when HTML semantics are insufficient. Use it for dynamic content, custom widgets, and when HTML elements don't convey their purpose. First preference should always be semantic HTML.

intermediate.question: What are ARIA landmarks and how do they help?
intermediate.answer: ARIA landmarks (role="banner", "navigation", "main", "complementary", "contentinfo") define regions of a page. Screen reader users can jump between landmarks to navigate efficiently.

intermediate.question: How do you handle dynamic content updates accessibly?
intermediate.answer: Use aria-live regions with appropriate politeness settings (polite, assertive, off). Announce changes in a way that doesn't disrupt the user. For critical updates, use role="alert".

intermediate.question: What is the accessible name computation?
intermediate.answer: The accessible name is how assistive technologies identify an element. It's computed from: 1) aria-labelledby, 2) aria-label, 3) associated label, 4) element's content, 5) title attribute. The first available source is used.

intermediate.question: How do you make a custom dropdown select accessible?
intermediate.answer: Use role="listbox" for the container, role="option" for items, aria-selected for current selection, aria-activedescendant on the input, manage keyboard interactions (arrow keys, Enter, Escape), and ensure focus management.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between aria-hidden and role="presentation"?
intermediate.answer: aria-hidden="true" removes an element and its children from the accessibility tree entirely. role="presentation" removes semantic meaning but keeps the element and its content accessible. Use aria-hidden for decorative content, role="presentation" for layout tables.

intermediate.question: How do you handle modal dialogs accessibly?
intermediate.answer: Use role="dialog" or role="alertdialog", aria-modal="true", aria-labelledby for title. Trap focus within the modal, manage focus on open/close, close with Escape key, and return focus to the triggering element.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the title attribute in accessibility?
intermediate.answer: The title attribute can provide additional information but has poor screen reader support and is not keyboard accessible on most elements. Use it only as a last resort for providing an accessible name.

intermediate.question: How does CSS affect accessibility?
intermediate.answer: CSS affects accessibility through: display:none vs visibility:hidden (hidden from all vs just visual), content order vs visual order, color contrast, focus indicators, responsive design, and media queries for reduced motion preferences.

advanced.question: How would you implement a fully accessible single-page application (SPA) router?
advanced.answer: Manage focus on route changes by focusing the main content area or a heading after navigation. Announce route changes with aria-live regions. Ensure proper heading hierarchy per page. Handle back/forward browser navigation. Manage focus during loading states. Use ARIA landmarks consistently.

advanced.question: What are the WCAG 2.2 new success criteria?
advanced.answer: WCAG 2.2 (2023) added: 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (AA), 2.4.12 Focus Not Obscured (AAA), 2.4.13 Focus Appearance (AAA), 2.5.7 Dragging Movements (AA), 2.5.8 Target Size (AA), and 3.2.6 Consistent Help (A). These address focus visibility and interaction accessibility.

advanced.question: How do you implement accessible drag-and-drop?
advanced.answer: Provide keyboard alternatives: use aria-grabbed, aria-dropeffect, and keyboard handlers for move operations. Implement drag with Enter/Space to pick up, arrow keys to move, and Enter to drop. Announce drag state changes. Ensure visual indicators for drop targets.

advanced.question: What is the accessibility tree and how does it differ from the DOM tree?
advanced.answer: The accessibility tree is a subset of the DOM that browsers expose to assistive technologies. It includes elements with semantic meaning, accessible names, roles, states, and properties. Elements hidden with aria-hidden or display:none are excluded. ARIA attributes modify the accessibility tree without changing the DOM.

advanced.question: How would you handle accessibility in a canvas-based application?
advanced.answer: Canvas content is not natively accessible. Solutions: provide off-screen DOM representations with ARIA attributes, use focusable canvas elements with keyboard events, implement custom hit regions, use the canvas element's fallback content for descriptions, or use SVG for accessible graphics.

advanced.question: What are the accessibility implications of Shadow DOM?
advanced.answer: Shadow DOM can create accessibility challenges because shadow boundaries can affect how assistive technologies traverse content. Use delegatesFocus for proper focus management, ensure ARIA attributes cross shadow boundaries properly, and maintain accessible names through slots.

advanced.question: How do you test accessibility with screen readers?
advanced.answer: Test with multiple screen readers: NVDA (Windows/Chrome), VoiceOver (Mac/Safari), JAWS (Windows). Navigate by heading, landmark, link, form control. Test all interactive patterns. Listen for announcements of state changes. Verify focus management. Test with images disabled.

advanced.question: What is cognitive accessibility and how do you address it?
advanced.answer: Cognitive accessibility addresses users with learning disabilities, memory impairments, and attention deficits. Strategies: consistent navigation, clear language, sufficient time limits, no unexpected changes, error prevention and recovery, simple layouts, readable fonts, and proper heading hierarchy.

advanced.question: How does the accessibility of a page change when using CSS display:contents?
advanced.answer: display:contents removes the element's box from the layout but keeps it in the accessibility tree. This can cause unexpected accessibility issues because elements like <ul> or <table> might lose their semantic roles while their children remain exposed.

advanced.question: What is WCAG-EM and how is it used?
advanced.answer: WCAG-EM (Website Accessibility Conformance Evaluation Methodology) is a structured approach for evaluating website accessibility. It covers: defining scope, exploring the website, selecting representative samples, auditing against WCAG, and reporting findings. It's the recommended methodology for comprehensive accessibility audits.
